culturalContext.context: Cappuccino was invented in northern Italy and is the defining morning drink of Italian café culture — always consumed before noon. This high-protein version uses vanilla protein powder, Greek yogurt, and espresso to create a muffin that fuels your morning without sacrificing flavor.

ingredients.name: Pasture Raised Large Brown Eggs, 12 count
Espresso Ground Coffee, 10 oz
ingredients.amount: 2 tbsp dissolved in 3 tbsp hot water

steps: Preheat oven to 375°F. Line a muffin tin.
Dissolve espresso in hot water and cool slightly.
Whisk eggs, Greek yogurt, applesauce, espresso, and honey until smooth.
Combine flour, protein powder, chia seeds, baking powder, and a pinch of salt.
Fold wet into dry — do not overmix.
Fold in chopped dark chocolate.
Fill muffin cups ¾ full. Bake 18-20 minutes until toothpick comes out clean.
Cool 5 minutes before removing. Freeze up to 1 month.
Protein per muffin: Greek yogurt ~6g + protein powder ~8g + eggs ~6g + chia ~2g = ~11-12g total.

name: Harcha

culturalContext.origin: Morocco — a staple Moroccan breakfast flatbread
culturalContext.context: Harcha is a thick pan-fried Moroccan semolina flatbread with a grainy, slightly crispy exterior and soft interior. Eaten across Morocco every morning with butter, honey, or jam and mint tea. The semolina gives it a distinct sandy-outside, pillowy-inside texture unlike any other flatbread.

steps: Mix semolina, baking powder, and a pinch of salt.
Add olive oil and rub into semolina until it resembles wet sand.
Add Greek yogurt and mix, adding water 1 tbsp at a time until a soft sticky dough forms.
Shape into a round disc about ½ inch thick.
Cook in a dry pan over medium-low heat 6-8 minutes per side until golden.
Serve warm with honey and olive oil.
Meal prep: refrigerate up to 3 days, reheat in dry pan.

name: Tuscan Tuna and White Bean Salad

culturalContext.origin: Tuscany, Italy — a classic peasant dish
culturalContext.context: Tonno e fagioli is one of Tuscany's most beloved simple dishes — a pantry meal requiring no cooking. Tuscans are nicknamed mangiafagioli (bean eaters) for their deep love of white beans. Tuna packed in olive oil was a pantry staple across coastal Tuscany, and this combination became a classic lunch. It represents cucina povera — the simple nourishing food of the Italian poor that became iconic.

ingredients.name: Solid White Tuna in Water, 5 oz
Organic Extra Virgin Olive Oil, 16.9 fl oz
Rosemary Olive Oil Woven Wheat Crackers, 9 oz

steps: Drain tuna and beans.
Combine tuna, beans, olives, and artichoke hearts in a bowl.
Dress with olive oil, lemon juice, and oregano. Toss gently.
Serve with rosemary olive oil crackers.
Meal prep: keeps in fridge up to 4 days.

name: Greek Horiatiki Village Salad

culturalContext.origin: Greece — the definitive Greek salad
culturalContext.context: Horiatiki (village salad) is Greece's national salad. The authentic version contains no lettuce — just chunky tomato, cucumber, green pepper, kalamata olives, and a thick slab of feta on top, dressed with only olive oil and dried oregano. No vinegar, no lemon. The simplicity is the point.

ingredients.name: Organic Extra Virgin Olive Oil, 16.9 fl oz

steps: Cut tomatoes into large wedges, place in a wide bowl.
Add olives and peppers.
Place feta slab on top — do not crumble, the traditional presentation is a whole piece.
Drizzle olive oil generously over everything.
Sprinkle dried oregano. Do not toss — serve as is.
Serve with warm naan.

name: Niçoise Salad

culturalContext.origin: Nice, Provence, southern France
culturalContext.context: Salade Niçoise is one of the great dishes of Provençal cuisine, named after the city of Nice on the French Riviera. The authentic version is a composed salad of raw vegetables with tuna, hard boiled eggs, and anchovies, dressed with olive oil. It became internationally famous and is one of the most nutritionally complete composed salads in existence.

ingredients.name: Solid White Tuna in Water, 5 oz
Pasture Raised Large Brown Eggs, 12 count
Organic Extra Virgin Olive Oil, 16.9 fl oz

steps: Hard boil eggs 8 minutes, cool and halve.
Arrange all components on a large plate — do not mix, compose separately.
Place tuna in center, arrange tomatoes, radishes, olives, beans, and eggs around it.
Add sardines if using.
Dress with olive oil and lemon juice — no vinegar in the authentic version.
Meal prep: keep components separate, assemble fresh.

name: Lebanese Fattoush Salad

culturalContext.origin: Lebanon and the Levant
culturalContext.context: Fattoush is Lebanon's bread salad — pita toasted until crispy and broken into pieces, tossed with chopped vegetables and a tangy sumac dressing. Sumac is one of the defining flavors of Levantine cuisine.

ingredients.name: Organic Extra Virgin Olive Oil, 16.9 fl oz

steps: Combine kale, tomatoes, and radishes in a large bowl.
Whisk lemon juice, olive oil, sumac, and a pinch of salt for dressing.
Toss vegetables with dressing.
Add pita chips just before serving — do not add early or they go soggy.
Top with feta if using.
Note: sumac available at Middle Eastern grocery stores and many large supermarkets for ~$3.

name: Mujaddara
region: Lebanon, Syria, and across the Levant

culturalContext.origin: Lebanon, Syria, and across the Levant — one of the oldest dishes in the world
culturalContext.context: Mujaddara appears in medieval Arabic cookbooks and is one of the oldest continuously prepared dishes on earth. It is considered the food of the poor across the Levant — simple, cheap, and deeply nourishing. The caramelized onion topping transforms the dish from simple to extraordinary.

ingredients.name: Organic Extra Virgin Olive Oil, 16.9 fl oz

steps: Cook lentils in 3 cups water with cumin 20 minutes until soft. Add rice last 10 minutes.
Caramelize onion blend in olive oil over medium-low heat 20-25 minutes — patience is essential, they must be deep golden brown.
Season lentil and rice mixture with salt, pepper, and extra cumin.
Serve topped generously with caramelized onions and a drizzle of olive oil.
Serve with yogurt on the side if desired.
Meal prep: refrigerates 5 days, freezes 2 months.

name: Moroccan Harira Soup

culturalContext.origin: Morocco — the national soup eaten to break the Ramadan fast
culturalContext.context: Harira is Morocco's most beloved soup, built on tomatoes, lentils, and chickpeas, spiced with cinnamon, ginger, and turmeric. Every Moroccan family has their own version. This vegetarian version is one of the most nutritionally complete soups in this collection.

ingredients.name: Diced Tomatoes with Basil, Garlic and Oregano, 14.5 oz
Fat Free Reduced Sodium Chicken Broth, 32 oz

steps: Heat olive oil, cook peppers & onions 5 minutes.
Add turmeric, cumin, and cinnamon, stir 30 seconds.
Add lentils, chickpeas/beans, tomatoes, and broth. Bring to boil.
Reduce heat, simmer 25-30 minutes until thick.
Finish with lemon juice. Taste and adjust.
Freezes beautifully — make a double batch.

name: Turkish Red Lentil Soup
region: Turkey — eaten nationwide at every meal

culturalContext.origin: Turkey — the most widely eaten soup in Turkish cuisine
culturalContext.context: Mercimek çorbası is Turkey's national soup — eaten for breakfast, lunch, and dinner. It is thick, smooth, and deeply warming. Every Turkish school, restaurant, and home serves it. The red lentils dissolve completely into a velvety puree.

ingredients.name: Fat Free Reduced Sodium Chicken Broth, 32 oz

steps: Heat olive oil, cook peppers & onions 4 minutes.
Add rinsed lentils, broth, cumin, and turmeric. Bring to boil.
Reduce heat, simmer 20 minutes until lentils completely dissolve.
Blend until completely smooth with immersion blender.
Stir in lemon juice, taste and adjust.
Serve with a drizzle of olive oil and warm naan.
Freezes perfectly.

name: Moroccan Chicken Tagine with Olives
culturalName: طاجين الدجاج بالزيتون · Tagine Djaj bil Zaytoun

culturalContext.origin: Morocco — one of the defining dishes of Moroccan cuisine
culturalContext.context: Tagine refers both to the conical clay cooking vessel and the slow-braised stews made in it. Chicken tagine with preserved lemon and olives is considered Morocco's most iconic dish. Served over couscous it is one of the most complete and satisfying meals in North African cuisine.

ingredients.name: Diced Tomatoes with Basil, Garlic and Oregano, 14.5 oz

steps: Season chicken with turmeric, cumin, cinnamon, and black pepper.
Sear in olive oil over high heat 3 minutes per side. Remove.
Cook peppers & onions 5 minutes. Add tomatoes, lemon juice, and olives.
Return chicken, cover and braise over low heat 25-30 minutes.
Prepare couscous per package directions.
Serve chicken and sauce over couscous.
Meal prep: refrigerates 4 days, freezes 2 months.

name: Greek Gigantes Plaki
region: Greece — a classic meze and main course

culturalContext.origin: Greece — eaten as meze, side dish, and main course
culturalContext.context: Gigantes plaki (baked giant beans) is one of the most beloved dishes in Greek cuisine — large white beans baked in a rich tomato sauce with olive oil and herbs until creamy inside. Eaten at room temperature as meze or warm as a main course with crusty bread.

ingredients.name: Diced Tomatoes with Basil, Garlic and Oregano, 14.5 oz
Organic Extra Virgin Olive Oil, 16.9 fl oz
Minced Garlic in Extra Virgin Olive Oil, 7 oz

steps: Preheat oven to 375°F.
Cook peppers & onions in olive oil 5 minutes. Add garlic 1 minute.
Add tomatoes, oregano, and artichoke hearts. Simmer 5 minutes.
Stir in beans gently, transfer to a baking dish.
Drizzle extra olive oil over top.
Bake 25-30 minutes until sauce is thick and slightly caramelized.
Serve warm or at room temperature with naan.
Improves overnight — excellent meal prep.

name: Lebanese Chicken Shawarma Bowl

culturalContext.origin: Lebanon and the Levant — Middle Eastern street food icon
culturalContext.context: Shawarma is one of the most eaten street foods in the Middle East and Mediterranean world — spiced meat cooked on a rotating vertical spit. It spread globally with Lebanese diaspora communities. This bowl version captures all the classic flavors without the wrap, making it naturally gluten-free.

steps: Toss chicken with turmeric, cumin, cinnamon, and olive oil.
Cook in a hot pan over high heat 4-5 minutes until slightly charred.
Cook brown rice per package directions.
Build bowl: rice base, chicken, olives, feta, and a generous dollop of tzatziki.
Meal prep: assemble without tzatziki, refrigerate 4 days. Add fresh when serving.

name: Algerian Couscous with Roasted Vegetables

culturalContext.origin: Algeria, Morocco, and Tunisia — North Africa's defining grain dish
culturalContext.context: Couscous is so culturally important that UNESCO added it to its Intangible Cultural Heritage list jointly for Algeria, Morocco, Tunisia, and Mauritania. Friday couscous — eaten after Friday prayers — is a sacred weekly ritual across North Africa.

ingredients.name: Diced Tomatoes with Basil, Garlic and Oregano, 14.5 oz

steps: Roast sweet potatoes at 400°F per package directions until caramelized.
Sauté peppers & onions in olive oil 5 minutes. Add spices, cook 1 minute.
Add tomatoes and beans, simmer 10 minutes.
Prepare couscous per package directions.
Serve vegetable stew over couscous.
Meal prep: keep stew and couscous separate, refrigerate 4 days.

name: Moroccan Lamb Kefta in Tomato Sauce
region: Morocco — a classic Moroccan family dish

culturalContext.origin: Morocco — eaten across the country at home and from street grills
culturalContext.context: Kefta are spiced ground meat patties — the Moroccan version of meatballs — seasoned with cumin, coriander, cinnamon, and herbs. Cooked in spiced tomato sauce they become kefta bil matisha. The warm spices — cinnamon and cumin — are distinctly Moroccan.

ingredients.name: Diced Tomatoes with Basil, Garlic and Oregano, 14.5 oz
Pasture Raised Large Brown Eggs, 12 count

steps: Mix lamb with cumin, cinnamon, turmeric, and a pinch of salt. Form into small oval patties.
Brown in olive oil 2 minutes per side. Remove.
In same pan combine diced tomatoes and salsa, simmer 5 minutes.
Return kefta to sauce, cover and simmer 15 minutes.
In last 5 minutes crack eggs directly into sauce to poach.
Prepare couscous per package directions.
Serve kefta and sauce over couscous.

name: Moroccan Vegetable Kefta

culturalContext.origin: Morocco — vegetarian adaptation of the classic kefta
culturalContext.context: Vegetarian kefta uses lentils as the base instead of meat, shaped into the same oval patties and cooked in the same spiced tomato sauce. The warm spice profile — cumin, cinnamon, turmeric — is fully authentic.

ingredients.name: Diced Tomatoes with Basil, Garlic and Oregano, 14.5 oz

steps: Mash cooked lentils with spices and masa mix until a workable mixture forms.
Shape into small oval patties.
Brown in olive oil 2-3 minutes per side until crispy. Remove.
In same pan simmer diced tomatoes 5 minutes.
Return patties to sauce, cover and cook 10 minutes.
Serve over couscous.

name: Israeli Baked Fish with Chermoula
region: Israel and North Africa — chermoula originates in Morocco

culturalContext.origin: Israel — drawing on North African Jewish culinary tradition
culturalContext.context: Chermoula is a North African herb and spice marinade — bright with lemon, cumin, and paprika. When Moroccan and North African Jews emigrated to Israel in the mid-20th century, they brought chermoula with them. Baked fish with chermoula is now one of the most iconic Friday night Shabbat dishes in Israeli Jewish homes.

ingredients.name: Minced Garlic in Extra Virgin Olive Oil, 7 oz
Organic Extra Virgin Olive Oil, 16.9 fl oz

steps: Preheat oven to 425°F.
Mix olive oil, lemon juice, garlic, cumin, turmeric, and lemon pepper into a paste.
Coat fish fillets generously with chermoula paste.
Bake 12-14 minutes until flaky.
Prepare couscous per package directions.
Serve fish over couscous with extra lemon wedges.

name: Spanish Lentejas
region: Spain — a staple of Spanish home cooking

culturalContext.origin: Spain — eaten in every Spanish household every season
culturalContext.context: Lentejas is one of Spain's most beloved comfort foods — a simple hearty stew eaten across all regions and social classes. There is a Spanish saying: 'lentejas, si quieres las comes y si no las dejas.' This vegetarian version uses smoked paprika which is essential to the authentic Spanish flavor.

ingredients.name: Diced Tomatoes with Basil, Garlic and Oregano, 14.5 oz
Fat Free Reduced Sodium Chicken Broth, 32 oz

steps: Heat olive oil, cook peppers & onions 5 minutes.
Add cumin and turmeric, stir 30 seconds.
Add rinsed lentils, tomatoes, and broth. Bring to boil.
Reduce heat, simmer 25-30 minutes until lentils are tender.
Finish with a squeeze of lemon.
Serve with crusty bread or naan.
Freezes perfectly.

name: Ribollita

culturalContext.origin: Tuscany, Italy — Florence and the surrounding countryside
culturalContext.context: Ribollita means 'reboiled' — made by taking leftover minestrone, adding stale bread to thicken it, and reboiling it. It is one of the great dishes of cucina povera and has become one of Tuscany's most iconic soups. The bread dissolves into a thick, porridge-like consistency. Best on the second or third day.

ingredients.name: Diced Tomatoes with Basil, Garlic and Oregano, 14.5 oz
Fat Free Reduced Sodium Chicken Broth, 32 oz
Organic Extra Virgin Olive Oil, 16.9 fl oz

steps: Heat olive oil, cook peppers & onions 5 minutes.
Add tomatoes, broth, beans, artichoke hearts, and oregano. Bring to boil.
Reduce heat, simmer 15 minutes.
Add kale, stir in and cook 10 minutes.
Tear bread into the soup, stir well — it will dissolve and thicken.
Simmer 5 more minutes, adjust seasoning.
Drizzle with olive oil before serving.
Best reheated the next day — the classic ribollita experience.
